data.title: Cullinan Therapeutics: Cash-Rich Biotech With Multiple Value Drivers
data.description:
Cullinan Therapeutics, Inc. (CGEM) is a clinical-stage drug developer that focuses on a wholly owned pipeline. They have bispecific T-cell engagers for autoimmune and blood cancer treatments, combined with a late-stage partnered lung cancer asset: zipalertinib. CGEM and Taiho have initiated a rolling NDA for zipalertinib for non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) and expect to finish the submission in Q1 2026. Thus, I think CGEM continues to make promising progress across its pipeline. And coupled with its robust balance sheet and reasonable valuation, I think the stock remains a great speculative “Buy” at these levels.

Promising Oncology Progress

Cullinan Therapeutics, Inc. is a clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company that develops oncology and immunology therapies. It was founded back in 2016 and is currently headquartered in Cambridge, Massachusetts. I previously covered CGEM in July last year with a “BUY” rating. Since then, the stock has appreciated by almost 65.6%, so I thought it was worthwhile writing an update on this name.

As a quick recap, CGEM’s pipeline has a wholly owned bispecific T cell engager program targeting autoimmune diseases and blood cancer. They also have a small-molecule program in lung cancer developed in partnership with Taiho Oncology, a subsidiary of the Japanese Taiho Pharmaceutical Co.

Having said that, their T cell engager program is a bispecific antibody-like drug that binds CD3 on T cells to activate them while simultaneously binding a disease target on the harmful cell. This way, CGEM can bring T cells into close contact and can kill the target cells. This program includes CLN-978 for autoimmune conditions and CLN-049 for acute myeloid leukemia (AML).

Moreover, CLN-978 is in Phase 1 for systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), rheumatoid arthritis (RA), and Sjögren’s disease (SjD), with initial data in 1H2026. In an earlier Phase 1 trial in B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma (B-NHL), CLN-978 showed that after dosing, B cells dropped quickly, which is expected if the drug successfully recruits T cells to kill CD19-positive B cells. Thus, it’s a promising early-stage asset that could eventually become a major value driver for the stock.

Longer-Term Bets

Similarly, many autoimmune diseases are driven by B cells (antibodies, antigen presentation, and inflammatory signaling). If CLN-978 can deplete the pathogenic B-cell population in a durable way, it can achieve better results than other drugs that only suppress inflammation. Also, this drug may have the advantage of being off-the-shelf, so it may be a ready-made drug without needing further personalization for its targets.

Aside from CLN-978, CGEM has CLN-049 as a bispecific T cell engager that brings T cells to kill FLT3, often expressed on leukemic blasts in AML. This candidate is studied in Phase 1 in relapsed/refractory (r/r) AML and myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS). So far, CLN-049 has received the FDA’s Fast Track Designation in r/r AML. This candidate might improve on or complement existing FLT3 tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs), with the potential to treat many AML patients. This is because FLT3 is present on a large fraction of AML blasts (80%), so it makes CLN-049’s MoA relevant for that demographic as well. In addition, FLT3 is expressed not only on blasts but also on leukemic stem cells, which, if eliminated, could support more durable remissions.

It’s worth mentioning that CLN-049 has shown early signs of durable remissions in a Phase 1 AML/MDS trial, especially with a target dose greater than 6 µg/kg. In 32 patients treated at these doses, 8 patients achieved complete remission (CR/CRh), 5 patients had a duration of response (DoR) of more than 16 weeks, 3 patients had a negative measurable residual disease (MRD), and achieved a DoR of more than 16 weeks, including 1 with more than 36 weeks. Lastly, 2 additional patients attained CR and could proceed to bone marrow transplant. So far, its safety profile seems manageable as well, which is another green flag for its follow-up trials.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, CGEM currently trades at a $729.0 million market cap, so by now it’s nearing that billion-dollar threshold. And its latest 10-Q from Q3 2025 shows its balance sheet holds $103.3 million in cash and $369.3 million in investments (short and long term). That amounts to about $472.6 million in liquid resources to continue funding its operations and R&D, against no financial debt. In fact, CGEM only has regular operating liabilities, and in total, its liabilities reached $32.8 million. So, its implied EV of just $256.4 million is actually quite compelling despite its seemingly loftier market cap.

Unfortunately, for now, CGEM doesn’t really generate any product revenues. This does make the stock inherently very speculative and entirely reliant on its R&D programs. Nor does CGEM anticipate any product sales in the near term either. So, as you might imagine, if their research ultimately never leads to a product approval, it could eventually erode substantial shareholder value over time through cash burn. I estimate that in Q3 2025 alone, they burned through $36.9 million. Note that I got this figure by simply adding its quarterly cash flows from operations and CAPEX, which also implies a manageable runway of 12.8 quarters.

But, over time, it could become an issue if they never reach a regulatory green light, so that’s why I can only lean towards a speculative “Buy” for now. Fortunately, by November 2025, CGEM and Taiho submitted a rolling NDA for zipalertinib to treat a subset of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). In my view, this is probably CGEM’s main value drivers, and it can be helpful for NSCLC patients with a specific mutation (ex20ins). Zipalertinib also has the FDA’s Breakthrough Therapy Designation (BTD), which enables the company to submit sections of the application as they are completed (rolling NDA).

Additionally, CGEM and Taiho are requesting accelerated approval and priority review for zipalertinib. This matters because CGEM’s management believes they can finalize zipalertinib’s NDA by Q1 2026. However, note that their request for accelerated approval means that the FDA may consider approval based on a surrogate endpoint. In previously treated, mutation-defined NSCLC, the typical surrogate endpoints are objective response rate (ORR). Often, the number of patients whose tumors are reduced meaningfully, and DOR are the key determinants of approval.

But note that zipalertinib’s NDA is based on the efficacy data from REZILIENT1, which was its Phase 1/2 trial as a monotherapy in adults with advanced/metastatic NSCLC (EGFR ex20ins) who had prior therapy. So, if zipalertinib receives an accelerated approval, it could make CGEM eligible for up to $130.0 million from Taiho tied to regulatory milestones with this program. And that figure alone would be equivalent to 17.8% of CGEM’s current market cap, which is why I feel it could be a major near-term catalyst. After that, I also think the rest of its pipeline with CLN-978 will become the main driver, as it’s also a wholly owned and internally developed asset.

Conclusion: Reiterated “Buy”

Overall, CGEM continues to make compelling R&D progress across all of its pipeline assets. For now, I feel the main variable lies with zipalertinib because if this candidate receives approval, it could also validate by extension the rest of its pipeline. It would also make CGEM eligible for additional milestone payments from Taiho, which would unlock immediate shareholder value. Naturally, this remains highly speculative because if zipalertinib receives a regulatory negative, the stock could quickly give back its recent gains. After all, the stock is already up 111% from its 2025 lows, so we can’t rule out a sharp retrace on any negative headlines at this point. But, for longer-term investors, I still feel CGEM remains a great speculative “Buy” as long as you manage your position sizing prudently.

data.title: AT&T Really Is Becoming A Growth Stock (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The Stock Is Back Where It Started Despite Better Numbers

It's been over a year since I asked the question, "Is AT&T Becoming A Growth Stock?". That was in December 2024 rolled out its 3-year plan to grow earnings and cash flow by continuing to aggressively pursue its strategy by expanding its wireless and fiber broadband businesses. It was also the first time AT&T (T) gave details on its plans to cut costs by phasing out its legacy copper wire based networks. The market seemed to initially like the story, taking the stock from $23.61 at the time of publication to just under $30.

Just before the stock hit these 4-year highs, AT&T announced a couple of big acquisitions. The first was a $5.75 billion deal to expand its fiber footprint by buying the mass market fiber business from Lumen (LUMN). The second was a $23 billion deal to purchase wireless spectrum from EchoStar (SATS). I discussed these deals in my most recent AT&T article in August 2025. Judging by the comments section of that article, some investors were nervous given the unprofitable acquisitions in AT&T's past and the fact that it would delay the company from hitting its 2.5 net debt/EBITDA leverage target, along with any raise in the $0.2775 quarterly dividend, now unchanged since April 2022. Still, these deals are different from say, DirecTV or WarnerMedia, because they provide the necessary capacity for AT&T to grow delivery of its core services. However, it still may take a few years (possibly to 2027 or 2028) before these acquisitions become accretive to earnings.

Anyway, it has been downhill for AT&T stock since September, basically round-tripping any gains since the December 2024 strategy rollout, making the Hold rating in my last article a good one. The declines might not be company specific, as Verizon (VZ) and T-Mobile (TMUS) have been even worse performers.

Things may have turned around for AT&T following the release of Q4 2025 earnings. For the full year of 2025, the company posted adjusted EPS of $2.12 and free cash flow of $16.6 billion, beating original 2025 guidance by $0.10 and $600 million, respectively. More importantly, AT&T announced 2026 EPS guidance of $2.25 - $2.35, and "double digit 3-year CAGR through 2028", which implies EPS of at least $2.82 in 2028. The company also announced free cash flow guidance of at least $18 billion in 2026, $19 B in 2027, and $21 B in 2028. The 2026 and 2027 numbers are each $1 billion higher than the company was guiding a year ago. The new guidance for 2028 shows accelerating FCF growth as both the capex to build out wireless and fiber capacity and the acquisitions begin to deliver growth.

The 10% EPD CAGR through 2028 is significantly better than analyst consensus, which is for EPS of $2.58 in 2028, for a 3-year CAGR of just 6.8%.

AT&T appears to be tweaking its strategy a bit to achieve this added growth. In earlier years, the company was focused on being more of a premium provider with full-price wireless service and fiber-only broadband. AT&T now seems more willing to sacrifice some price for customer retention and continues to expand its wireless broadband offering. This not only expands AT&T's addressable market to more mid-tier customers but also helps accelerate the phase-out of legacy copper wire networks.

To highlight the growth areas of the company, AT&T is changing its domestic segments for financial reporting. From 2026 forward, the segments will be a new technology "Advanced Connectivity" segment and an old technology "Legacy" segment. (The small Latin America segment will continue as-is.) While this does not change overall financials for the company, it clarifies the growing areas and makes it easier to track progress on the phase-out of the legacy businesses. This should help AT&T get a growth stock valuation closer to T-Mobile once the market sees a clear path to eliminating the legacy businesses that dragged overall company growth down.

Subtle Strategy Shift

In the mobile market, AT&T has historically focused on being a premium provider, avoiding promotional pricing, and minimizing churn. Their growth has been very steady, consistently adding around 400,000 postpaid phone accounts per quarter for the last 3 years. This is above Verizon but less than T-Mobile. In the past year, however, churn has increased and is approaching the 1% level, going from the lowest to the highest of the three. Based on the earnings call, the company is planning to sacrifice revenue per user (ARPU) growth and pursue some lower-end customers, shifting the focus a bit to volume from price.

AT&T has also been focusing on growing the number of customers bundling broadband and mobile services. The company tracks "Converged Customers", which is the percentage of fiber broadband users with AT&T mobile. This has grown from 38.8% in 2023 to 40% in 2024 and 42% in 2025. Management expects to increase this to 50% or more. The ability to cross sell mobile service is another benefit of the organic fiber expansion, as well as the Lumen deal, which expands the fiber footprint to new geographies. The company expects to have the capacity to serve 40 million locations with fiber by the end of this year, up from 32 million at the end of 2025 and on the way to 60 million by 2030.

Broadband has also been the area of another strategic pivot - the growth of wireless internet service. A few years ago, AT&T pushed fiber exclusively due to its higher data speeds, but as others, like T-Mobile, began capturing low- to mid-tier customers who don't need the bandwidth, AT&T was quickly losing customers using legacy technologies like DSL. As recently as 2023, the company's non-fiber losses were as high as the net fiber adds. After the introduction of AT&T Internet Air in 2023, uptake has been popular and adds are approaching similar numbers to fiber, which is around 1 million customers per year. This has greatly stemmed the number of non-fiber losses, which for now still include legacy technology like DSL and copper voice-only lines.

Internet Air has also turned out to be essential in phasing out of the legacy services. AT&T has received approval from the FCC to discontinue copper service in 30% of the footprint this year and expects to end most of the remaining service by 2029. This is a large part of the $4 billion of cost savings the company expects to deliver over the next 3 years.

New Segment Reporting

AT&T is changing reporting segments in 2026 to better highlight the growth businesses within the company. Instead of the previous Communications, Business Wireline, and Consumer Wireline, the company will now have two domestic segments - Advanced Connectivity and Legacy. The small Latin America segment will not change. The company has recast 2023-2025 results, showing the contrasting growth and decline of each. The Advanced Connectivity segment has grown sales by 3.3% and EBITDA by 6.3% each year for the past two years, while the Legacy segment has shown steep declines. The company expects EBITDA of this segment to go negative in 2027 as costs to phase out these services exceed declining revenue. However, within a few more years, the costs should go away as well.

The Advanced Connectivity segment has EBITDA growth rates that approach T-Mobile, although there is still some room to improve. Growth is much better than the full company numbers for Verizon.

Capital Management

As seen on the long-term outlook above, AT&T will continue to be a big capital spender, at $23-$24 billion per year from 2026-2028, slightly above the $22 billion each year in 2024 and 2025. Based on comments in the earnings, call, I would expect this to taper off to $18-$19 billion in 2029 and 2030.

Despite the higher capex, free cash flow is still expected to grow from the $16.3 billion delivered in 2025. FCF should be at least $18 billion in 2026, $19 billion in 2027, and $21 billion in 2028. FCF should really take off in 2029 and beyond as most of the legacy services are phased out, capex declines, and the company utilizes its new capacity from the Lumen and EchoStar deals.

AT&T got down to a net debt/EBITDA leverage of 2.53 by the end of 2025, the lowest of the three telcos. Leverage will temporarily increase to 3.2 with the planned acquisitions before declining to 3.0 by the end of 2026 and back to 2.5 by 2029.

The company expects to buy back about $8 billion per year of stock for the next few years. The dividend is expected to be unchanged at $1.11 per year through 2028. The dividend will consume $7.7 billion of cash in 2026, but the buybacks should reduce share count by about 1 billion shares to 6.1 billion by the end of 2028, meaning the dividend should consume around $6.8 billion of cash by the end of 2028.

Doing the math, we see that free cash flow after buybacks and dividends is about $2.3 billion in 2026, but this increases to $6.2 billion by the end of 2028. I expect the excess cash to be used to pay down debt during this time frame, but after 2028, there should be sufficient free cash flow to raise the dividend by as much as $1 per year, or 90% from current levels.

Valuation

I updated the comparison from the August article using management's forecast of 10% EPS CAGR over the next three years. Verizon and T-Mobile have not reported at the time of writing. Earnings for those companies each year is based on the analyst consensus from the Seeking Alpha earnings pages. While all three stocks have gotten cheaper since August, Verizon is still expensive relative to growth with a PEG ratio over 2. With AT&T's new outlook, it is catching up to T-Mobile on a growth-adjusted valuation basis.

Beyond 2028, AT&T should close the gap with T-Mobile as it starts adding fiber customers from the Lumen deal and wireless mobile and internet customers from the EchoStar spectrum. AT&T's cost structure will also more closely resemble T-Mobile's as it exits its legacy copper wire networks. While T-Mobile looks like the cheapest Telco stock to buy now, AT&T is also a Buy as it has already narrowed the gap on PEG ratio based on the next three years of growth.

Conclusion

AT&T's strategy has not changed since the rollout at the end of 2024. The company remains focused on growing its core business of mobile and broadband services. However, since then, the company has acquired more fiber and wireless spectrum to provide the needed capacity for growth. AT&T has also tweaked its high-end market strategy to bring in more mid-tier customers with mobile pricing and wireless internet. The fixed wireless move will also help the company accomplish its exit from legacy copper wire service within a few years, lowering costs.

Prior to the Q4 earnings release, the stock had traded all the way back down to where it was at the time of the December 2024 investor day. With both the growth outlook and the plans to deliver it improved since then, the stock is worthy of an upgrade to Buy.

data.title: General Mills Has Seen Excessive Gloom And Doom
data.description:
In late October, I upgraded General Mills, Inc. (GIS) to a buy rating. I expressed my belief that better days were ahead, as there were some signs of stabilization and the valuation was near multiyear lows. Since then, the stock has continued to slump, as it's now down around 9% since the publication of my update. The company reported their most recent results mid last month, and so today, we'll see if I have indeed jumped the gun with my upgrade.

Below, it is shown that Q2 was painful but a decent quarter for the company. Organic sales declines have slowed, and they were able to beat top and bottom line expectations. The company also laid out their priorities for FY2026 that make sense, and if they are able to execute, it seems the long term outlook is solid. General Mills failed to deliver a beat and raise, but full-year guidance still shows that the worst is likely over. Especially when considering that the valuation remains at rock-bottom levels, I still see brighter days ahead for the company and for the stock. Therefore, I'm reiterating my buy rating.

Q2 Sales Analysis

For FY2026 Q2, General Mills posted rather worrying top-line results. They had net sales of $4.861 billion, which represents a decline of 7% YoY. As you can see in the chart above, the overall downtrend in growth doesn't seem to have ended, and in fact, Q2 represented the worst sales decline in the past few years. Therefore, it seems that their business is seeing a worsening trajectory. Still, General Mills beat analyst expectations by $78.14 million, and so results were better than feared.

While reported declines worsened, as a potentially encouraging sign, their organic net sales actually only decreased by 1% YoY. That is an improvement from the previous quarter's 3% decline. Since organic sales are usually a better reflection of a company's core business activity, I would say that there are actually some encouraging signs that aren't shown in the reported GAAP figure. As you can see in the breakdown of sales growth above, acquisitions & divestitures were the key contributor to the reported sales weakness. In organic terms, there was some mild weakness in price/mix, perhaps as a result of consumers choosing cheaper goods, but volume held steady as a sign of stability and resilience.

Before moving on, it is worth mentioning there was some notable difference between their North America sales overall and international sales. In North America, their best performing segment by organic net sales growth was Pet at 1%, while the worst was their Retail at -3%. For their international segment, they saw 4% of organic growth, and so there is quite a contrast. In my view, this continues to reflect relative consumer weakness in the U.S. as a result of sticky inflation near 3% as well as volatile tariff policy from the Trump Administration. The underperformance in their domestic market is clearly a drag on performance and that is potentially a concern since the North American market makes up the vast majority of their sales.

Pain In Their Margins

Overall, there is significant pain seen in the company's margins. For adjusted gross margin, they saw 150 basis points of contraction, and they mainly attributed this to higher input costs. When companies like General Mills state that there were higher input costs, it more than likely means that tariffs have eroded the company's cost control. Therefore, it really is a double whammy that tariffs are both hitting consumer confidence and cost control measures. This leads to a significant erosion of profitability.

For adjusted operating margin, things are also a bit worrying. For Q2, they saw 290 basis points of contraction YoY. They attributed this to higher SG&A expenses as a percentage of net sales, and so there are signs that corporate efficiency is also weaker than desirable.

At the end of the day, General Mills reported an adjusted EPS of $1.10. While EPS declines accelerated from 19.6% in Q1 to 21.4%, note that they were able to still beat expectations by a respectable $0.07. Therefore, even though profitability is definitely shaky, Q2 was still arguably an outperforming quarter for the bottom line.

Strategy Is Reasonable

General Mills laid out their FY2026 priorities, and I would say that they make sense considering where they are right now. The first priority, returning North America Retail to volume growth is obvious crucial. This segment is their largest by a considerable margin, and so they would be wise to heavily invest in this area to boost performance over time.

Their second priority, to accelerate North America Pet growth, is also a very intuitive decision. As stated earlier, this segment was their strongest by organic net sales growth in North America, and so it makes sense that they double down here and ride some of the momentum they have been seeing.

Lastly, for their third priority, they are focused on driving efficiencies. After the margin discussion above, I believe that this is obviously an important area to focus on. Overall, I would say that General Mills has laid out their strategy quite nicely. Now, it's likely a matter of execution, and that's equally as important.

Capital Returns Suggest Cautiousness

While Seeking Alpha rates their dividend very favorably, as shown on the right, there are some signs that the company is a bit cautious with their capital returns as a whole. In Q2, General Mills basically didn't buy back any stock, while in the year ago quarter, they bought back $300 million. For dividends, their total payout dropped from $338 million to $327.9 million. While that's probably just because of a decrease in the number of shares rather than a cut in the dividend, I would say that overall, the company seems a bit hesitant to distribute capital in this tough macro environment. The company may also be using capital to fund their strategy that I discussed above, and so it's not that big of a surprise that they have adjusted their capital return policy accordingly.

Outlook Reiterated

Despite beating on both the top and bottom line in Q2, General Mills left their FY2026 guidance unchanged, and so there may be a bit of disappointment. In my previous article, I already discussed the figures shown in the chart above and concluded that guidance is a bit shaky, but the worst could also be over. Here, I'll just briefly highlight some points.

Back in FY2025, they had organic net sales growth of -2%, and so FY2026 is setting up to be a mild improvement. While the above ranges for adjusted operating profit growth and adjusted EPS growth represent deteriorations from the previous year, as stated in their guidance above, there are notable impacts from divestitures & acquisitions as well as from normalization of corporate incentive expense. When excluding these headwinds and effects, declines may actually be less than in FY2025. Lastly, note that both organic net sales and adjusted EPS guidance were above consensus expectations despite being unchanged.

Valuation At Rock Bottom Levels

I concluded that General Mills' valuation was cheap in my previous update when the forward P/E ratio was at 13.41. Since then, there has been further contraction to the current reading of 12.16. I believe that despite the substantial amount of pain the company is going through right now, the valuation does offer a notable amount of opportunity.

Firstly, in Q2, organic sales declines slowed as a promising sign, and despite the margin contraction, they still beat expectations on the bottom line. Looking forward, the company has laid out a clear and sensible strategy in my view and if they are able to execute, I would say that they are positioned relatively well. Furthermore, while they left full year guidance unchanged, FY2026 is still shaping up to be a better year for organic sales growth and potentially for adjusted EPS growth as well when excluding the headwinds stated above.

Therefore, not much has changed with regard to my view that brighter days are ahead. With that in mind, I believe the extremely depressed valuation indicates that there is considerable opportunity at the stock's current levels.

Recap

There is no question that the company is currently in a state of pain due to the macro environment and tariff headwinds. That said, I would say that there has been too much gloom and doom. As mentioned above, I stated that better days are ahead in my previous update. Today, I found Q2 results that were decent considering the state of things and FY2026 guidance that was reiterated. Therefore, I see no reason for the valuation to have contracted further since October. In my view, the story for General Mills and its stock has remained unchanged, and so I'm reiterating my buy rating.

data.title: Fed Holds Steady And Signals Longer-Term Easing Bias
data.description:
Amid a rush of domestic and foreign policy developments that have rattled markets – including the potential for another U.S. government shutdown – the Federal Reserve held steady on U.S. monetary policy.

At the January meeting, the Fed held interest rates steady in a range of 3.5%–3.75% after cutting by 75 basis points over the three previous meetings. Despite the hold, the forward guidance language in the Fed’s policy statement suggests most officials agree that further easing will eventually be appropriate, although there is no sense of urgency. We expect the Fed will remain on hold until later this year, when rate cuts are likely to resume.

Risks diminishing on both sides of the Fed’s mandate

Ahead of the meeting, public remarks by Fed officials suggested that previously divergent views on inflation and labor market risks have converged somewhat.

Recent data point to solid economic momentum, improved labor market stability, and relaxing inflationary pressures. This has helped lower the risks to both sides of the Fed’s mandate (maximum employment and price stability) and thus supported the hold-steady approach.

At the press conference, Fed Chair Jerome Powell didn’t appear in a hurry to cut interest rates. He emphasized that rates are now closer to neutral, while data have strengthened the U.S. growth outlook and reduced downside labor market risk. He emphasized that monetary policy is in a good place to react to incoming developments.

The one surprise of January’s meeting was Governor Christopher Waller dissenting in favor of a quarter-point rate cut, along with Governor Stephen Miran (whose dissent was expected).

Waller has been advocating for rate cuts in light of what he describes as risks to labor markets along with moderating inflation once tariff effects are excluded. However, his public comments ahead of the January meeting did not appear to make a strong case for another rate cut this soon.

U.S. inflation set to moderate despite firm GDP growth

Our outlook for further rate cuts later this year is based in part on inflation trends. Although core inflation is running around 3% now (versus the Fed’s target of 2%), several forces could help lower inflation even if real GDP growth remains firm.

AI implementation and investment, which accelerated in 2025, have coincided with (and helped drive) higher productivity growth and moderating unit labor cost inflation.

In time, this should help tame price inflation. Furthermore, tariff-related price increases also appear to have mostly run their course. If inflation data continues to ease, the Fed is likely to gradually ease monetary policy as well.

Broader macroeconomic trends inform our U.S. inflation outlook. In our latest Cyclical Outlook, “Compounding Opportunity,” we discuss how U.S. policy pivots and tariff-related costs prompted a surge in AI adoption and investment as companies aggressively defended margins.

Resilient aggregate growth has masked winners and losers, and labor markets have been a key loser. Indeed, as real labor income growth has slowed, the labor market, rather than consumer prices, has realized a greater-than-expected portion of the tariff-related adjustment. This has two longer-term implications for consumer price inflation.

First, although estimates suggest that only around 50% of total tariff costs have been passed on to U.S. consumer goods prices, we think these tariff-related price hikes have largely run their course as the downward adjustment in unit labor costs helped make up the difference for companies. This means we are probably at or close to peak goods inflation.

Second, AI-related trends appear to be reducing labor’s share of income. In the third quarter, it fell to the lowest level in the history of recorded data, which spans almost 80 years.

We expect continued AI implementation in 2026 (implementation actually remains fairly low in absolute terms), which will likely keep productivity outpacing nominal wage growth – while unit labor cost inflation sinks below levels consistent with the Fed’s target, and helps moderate price inflation over time.

U.S. housing market dynamics also support disinflation. Slowing household formation (possibly due in part to changes in immigration policy and related demographic trends) coupled with greater multifamily housing supply, especially in the Sun Belt cities, is softening rental demand while housing supply expands.

This has reduced nationwide new lease rental inflation below owners’ equivalent rent (OER) for the past year, after it materially outpaced average rents in the wake of the pandemic.

New lease rents are now more aligned with the broader measure of OER, which reflects average rents for both new leases and renewals across the U.S. economy. Without more need for average rents to catch up, OER inflation is now poised to undershoot pre-pandemic levels as market rental inflation remains subdued.

Key takeaway: Steady Fed awaits clear signals in the data before it resumes easing

January’s meeting reinforces a familiar Fed narrative: The central bank collectively sees policy as restrictive enough to cool inflation, and flexible enough to absorb shocks as officials wait for clearer confirmation before easing further.

With inflation pressures broadly receding – helped by productivity gains, fading tariff effects, and softer housing costs – the central bank is likely to stay on hold for now and resume rate cuts later this year. Unless growth weakens materially or inflation reaccelerates, the next phase of policy is likely to be gradual, measured easing rather than a rapid pivot.

Disclosures

All Investments contain risk and may lose value.

Statements concerning financial market trends or portfolio strategies are based on current market conditions, which will fluctuate. There is no guarantee that these investment strategies will work under all market conditions or are appropriate for all investors and each investor should evaluate their ability to invest for the long term, especially during periods of downturn in the market. Outlook and strategies are subject to change without notice.

data.title: BUI's Comeback Setup: Income Today, Tailwinds Tomorrow
data.description:
The BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure & Power Opportunities Trust (BUI) is an active hybrid portfolio, combining defensive utilities exposure with infra and power themes. Although the hybrid methodology implies greater growth than utilities and lower volatility than energy/infra, performance in the recent past has been lackluster on both counts. This is attributable primarily to BUI's currency exposures in a strong USD environment, among other factors.

I expect this underperformance to change in 2026 as the USD consolidates or weakens, providing relative tailwinds in an environment where utilities, infra and power sectors are likely to see decent growth. The option layer could add income (although BUI does not aggressively use this layer). BUI's income strength also adds to its Buy appeal.

The Proposition - Enhanced Utilities

BUI is an active portfolio that should be imagined like an enhanced utilities strategy. The typical utilities portfolio provides defensive equity exposure, focusing on dividends while keeping volatility low. BUI uses this profile as a base and adds a decent amount of infrastructure and power bets. Sectoral exposure beyond utilities adds higher growth potential, inflation pass-through and better performance in reflationary cycles. But it also introduces cyclicality and greater political and regulatory risks. That makes the combination more real-asset-like and less bond-like than utilities alone.

The current sector mix supports that enhanced utilities positioning well. Utilities are still more than half of the portfolio, retaining the defensive and income oriented core. However, the ~14% in energy and ~13% in capital goods signals meaningful exposure to power generation, grid equipment, and infrastructure buildout rather than just regulated rate-based businesses. The transportation sleeve (~9%) adds inflation-linked assets like rail, toll roads, and logistics players. However, the balance ensures the drift into cyclicals is not significant.

The BUI portfolio is also meaningfully diversified outside the US (around ~40% of the portfolio, and ~30% ex-US/Canada). This lowers sensitivity to the US rate regime, diversifies regulatory risks, and also capitalizes on Canada and Europe's renewables and electrification related energy transformation. BUI is therefore far removed from being just a US bond substitute to a diversified, income-oriented real assets fund. The large cap focus (~92% large caps in the portfolio) keeps the hunt for growth and reflationary stability from turning speculative and retains the utilities' defensive traits to a large extent. It also makes it easier to write options on (more predictable share price movements) - something BUI does to enhance income, although moderately. The covered call layer accounts for less than 10% of the overall portfolio by value. This is not aggressive call writing; it ensures upside capture is intact and only minor but more reliable income is added. Also, I don't expect infrastructure and utility stocks to generate a lot of premiums, so the income enhancement is not likely to be eye-catching, but consistent and reliable.

BUI's overall structure is therefore geared for income first, smoother returns next, and greater upsides last. Investors seeking income (without MLP/K-1 headaches) without heightened credit risks should find BUI's positioning valuable. Cash yields will be higher than utility vehicles, while the volatility is dampened compared to infra ETFs.

The top 10 holdings reinforce BUI's positioning as an enhanced utilities play rather than a pure defensive fund. Core regulated large caps like NextEra, Duke, Southern, AEP, and National Grid anchor cash flow stability, while names such as TC Energy, Williams, and Cheniere add midstream and LNG exposure tied to global energy plays. Union Pacific introduces inflation-linked transport infrastructure, and Aena adds regulated airport infrastructure. The mix shows a tilt toward large, systemically important operators where earnings are asset-backed but still benefit from electrification, energy security, and infrastructure investment themes.

Performance Shows Cracks

So investors who want to enhance income, boost total returns over utilities, and limit drawdowns below infra enter BUI, and this is what they see. Over the past 5 years, BUI has underperformed even pure utility strategies in terms of total returns. And drawdowns were sharper than pure infra plays. BUI's income promise is the only parameter that has lived up to the proposition. At $0.14 dividends monthly, the yield translates to over 6% annually - a far superior income tool compared to IDU's 2-3% yield range.

The disappointment with respect to drawdowns and total returns holds crucial clues that help extrapolate BUI's forward expectations. The first drag in BUI is because of the option layer. Currently it hovers around 10%, and while this could be dynamically and actively moved up or down, the odds of lower coverage look higher judging by how the drawdown cushion is missing in 2022-23. A well covered portfolio should at least see limited but some outperformance in drawdowns through a higher amount of premiums received. So, while the covered call layer could be capping BUI's total returns, it should not be the most dominant drag. For context, if the broader utilities strategies rallied by ~55% (close to real total returns over the last 5 years) and 10% of BUI's portfolio completely returned zero because of the option cap, the remaining 90% should have still captured 90% of the ~55% rally, i.e. returned closer to 50% than the ~46% returns we see today. And this is extremely conservative math I have illustrated for context.

The fact that the utilities dominant stance drags BUI's performance compared to infra strategies' marginally higher returns is understandable, but in the last 5 years there is little outperformance in infra too. So, that sectoral tilt also does not explain the underperformance. Geographic drag could be a factor, particularly given USD was strong in 2022-25.

Inefficient FX hedging alone could be a factor explaining the performance drag. BUI has ~35% exposure to foreign currencies. BUI's currency exposure operates at two levels. Because of global exposure, some holdings are listed in non-US markets and report in local currencies, so their share prices and dividends have to be translated to USD at the fund level. More importantly, many of these non-US utilities and infra companies generate a majority of their revenues outside the US. The tariffs, costs, and allowed returns are set in local currencies. Even stable local currency earnings translate into weaker USD results when the dollar strengthens. For example, holdings like National Grid (U.K.), Aena (Spain), and TC Energy (Canada) earn much of their regulated or contracted cash flow in pounds, euros, and Canadian dollars. When the U.S. dollar strengthens, those otherwise stable local earnings and dividends translate into fewer dollars at the fund level, creating a performance headwind.

Extrapolating Ahead

From the portfolio structure to actual performance outcomes, it is clear what BUI needs to outperform. It needs an environment where utilities continue to do well. That generally translates to low or stable rates. It definitely needs a weakening USD. A weakening dollar expectation is likely to work out in 2026, with growth slowing in the near term and labor market uncertainties pressuring the USD. Usually, lower rates compared to the rest of the world are a drag for the USD too.

As the USD weakens (or consolidates), BUI's portfolio will gain a relative advantage in 2026. Lower rates are also supportive for utilities in general too. So, overall, I expect BUI's gap with pure-play utilities or infra strategies to diminish in 2026.

The gap between infrastructure's growth expectations and utilities' defensive characteristics wasn't seen much in the last cycle. With rates easing and utilities getting a valuation boost (plus expansion in a predictable low-rate environment), the convergence between infra and utilities is likely to remain. So the infra and utilities diversification is unlikely to show up as near-term benefits in BUI. However, both sectors will likely be in a supportive environment that should drive decent total returns. In other words, BUI should do relatively well (at least close the gap versus pure play utilities or infra) and absolutely well because the sectoral tilt should ensure a decent yield and total returns. This is despite the diversification benefits still not showing up.

What finally tilts BUI to a Buy is that while delivering similar total returns, it should continue to deliver much higher income. This is somewhat enabled by an option layer that enhances the income aspect, particularly in flat or weaker markets. This is not a huge enhancer given weak coverage, but it should add precious basis points.

Therefore, despite lackluster performance in the recent past, BUI qualifies as a Buy on forward expectations. I would certainly position it as a satellite allocation in income portfolios primarily driven by its higher yields, in an expected regime of relative tailwinds that should at least ensure total returns similar to IDU or IGF.

data.title: Constellation Software: One Of The Last Places AI Entrepreneurs Will Target
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Giverny Capital Asset Management Q4 2025 Letter.

The fifth member of the laggards in 2025 was Constellation Software (CSU), another business that we have owned since our inception and that had been among our best stocks for most of that time. Constellation declined 22% for the year, even though I believe earnings will rise by nearly 20% for the full year. [The company reports full year results in early February]. The stock continued its steep decline in the first weeks of 2026.

Software businesses are perceived as facing an existential threat from AI models that can write software faster than humans can. AI-written software cannot yet replace existing products, but the market is looking ahead and saying: that day is coming.

In response, I'll argue that Constellation is a particular flavor of software company. It owns hundreds of small firms that have created bespoke products that customers use to run their operations. Scores of golf courses around the country run on various permutations of Constellation products, as do public transit systems, truck fleets, food service operations, hospitals and medical offices, and many others. No one product or customer represents a hefty amount of revenue. Each customer tends to have a unique operating system, even when they are in a similar enterprise. The software is not necessarily complex, but it is specific to its user. Most users also depend on Constellation for support, as a golf course or city bus system typically lacks sophisticated tech resources in house.

In sum, Constellation strikes me as one of the last places AI entrepreneurs will target. You'd be fighting for small pots of revenue with high customization and support needs. To date, AI software has proven buggy and not good at self-repair, in part because the AI models don't train on mistakes, so they don't know how to respond to them. AI software can also hallucinate and cause errors that people must then find and fix.

Nevertheless, investors have abandoned Constellation over the past six months. It further hurt that the brilliant founder-CEO Mark Leonard retired abruptly in September for health reasons. Constellation now trades at the cheapest price relative to earnings that I can recall. We're very much living in a momentum-based market environment, but if Constellation's earnings keep growing 15% to 20%, the stock price eventually will reflect that. For now, software businesses are radioactive.

Disclosures

The performance returns presented herein are those of a Poppe family account (the "Portfolio") that serves as the model portfolio of Giverny Capital Asset Management LLC ("GCAM"). The Portfolio is managed in accordance with the investment strategy that GCAM employs for its client accounts; however, the performance of a client account may differ from that of the Portfolio due to account size, client-specific guidelines or restrictions, tax considerations, cash flows into and out of the account and timing of transactions and other factors. Performance returns of the Portfolio as of the most recent quarter end can be found at www. givernycam. com/performance.

The views expressed herein are those of GCAM as of the date of this letter and are subject to change without notice.

GCAM makes no representations or warranties regarding the completeness or accuracy of any information contained herein, and does not guarantee that any forecast, projection or opinion will be realized. This letter is presented for informational purposes only, and the information herein is not intended, and should not be construed, as investment advice or as an offer or recommendation to buy or sell any security.

All investments involve risk and may lose value. For a discussion of risks, see Item 8 of GCAM's Form ADV Brochure.

Certain statements herein are "forward looking statements. "

Forward-looking statements are subject to risks, uncertainties and other factors that could cause actual results to differ materially from future results expressed or implied by such forward-looking statements. Readers should carefully consider such factors. Forward-looking statements speak only as of the date on which such statements are made; GCAM undertakes no obligation to update any forward-looking statements to reflect events or circumstances after the date of such statements.

data.title: QuantumScape: No Longer An Option, Now A Blueprint
data.description:
QuantumScape's (QS) price action suggests a good zone for an opportunistic Buy on the technical progress made. For a pre-revenue stock, measuring narrative progress is a difficult task. I have tried to formalize my arguments through a measurable framework for progress in terms of how many commercialization milestones have been de-risked over time. While the absolute framework could be up for debate, it provides useful insight into the magnitude of progress made.

I anchor my observations around Q2 2023, when the stock was primarily valued as a lab optionality. At the time, manufacturing planning was largely conceptual, and automotive partnerships were still exploratory. Markets were at a stage when they were exiting the rate-shocked lows of 2022-23, not really a market that was still fully rewarding growth. The 2025 lows and subsequent highs were distorted by market conditions, first going through a panic crash in April 2025, followed by a narrative supportive phase where several pre-revenue narrative bets rallied sharply.

As the momentum has slowed now, a comparison with Q2 2023 as an anchor should provide a cleaner comparison of progress made and how much the valuations have evolved since. I feel that this 2-3 year gap also helps filter out short-term market noise and narrative swings, providing a more realistic view of structural progress than comparisons driven by recent sentiment alone.

In terms of share prices alone, it is only up by over 20% since Q2 2023. Even the market cap is up by 67% only. That seems reasonable compared to progress made during this time. And that assessment includes any potential impact of time-to-market expectations that may have reset during roughly the past couple of years.

Progress Made Over Time

The summary of progress made between Q2 2023 and today is that what was once a question around arriving at a real product has now transformed into a real battery company answering manufacturing at scale questions. In 2023, QuantumScape was in the process of installing Raptor equipment and talking about Cobra as a future higher throughput process. They were running the early QS-0 pre-production line. This was a stage where the focus was on being able to build these batteries outside the lab. Today, QuantumScape has clearly moved into an early factory workflow stage. They have now shipped Cobra-based QSE-5 B1 samples, installed key equipment on the more automated Eagle pilot line, and even partnered with Murata and Corning to scale production of the ceramic separator. QS's earlier narrative implied they would have to solve large scale manufacturing mostly on their own. They are clearly leaning on industrial manufacturing experts, lowering execution risks. We are still not talking about yield rates and volumes, but QuantumScape has clearly moved out of the lab and is addressing manufacturing at scale questions.

We also are starting to see battery performance data being collected. QS batteries were used in a Ducati V21L electric race motorcycle demonstration, and greater field testing is being discussed. Again, we do not have durability or cycle life data, but we are seeing real-world validation. That's progress from where we were in 2023, when unit testing in the lab was in play.

The automotive customer-level engagement has also materially progressed. The Volkswagen Group is tied to the Ducati demo. An OEM is in a formal joint development agreement, and a large automaker is mentioned as being in active engagement. This is progress to the formal evaluation stage, although not yet production contracts or agreements.

In Q3 2025, QS reported $12.8m in customer billings. Though not a proof of commercialization yet, even minor payments for developmental work is progress from no customer revenues or billings in 2023. The runway has also received a boost with $1b liquidity reported in Q3 2025, and runway estimates extended till around 2030.

De-Risking Framework

My de-risking framework attempts to quantify progress to check whether the ~22% change in share prices over the past couple of years is too much. Not all milestones matter equally for valuations, so weights have been assigned to each factor that should be tracked for a pre-revenue industrial tech company. The exact weights I assign to each risk or stage of progress may be debatable, but I have tried to keep things conservative and hope to quantify the progress, even in a broad range.

For QS, two broad questions are what defines the risks to commercialization - whether the tech can be manufactured at automotive scale, and how long and how uncertain the path to commercialization is. Economics beyond commercialization is a different stage for investments altogether.

I have kept the two most relevant broad questions today in mind and assigned a high weightage to manufacturing uncertainties - because the ability to produce batteries that work at scale with consistent quality and yield ultimately decides whether the technology can become a viable business proposition. Automaker validation is another critical area because eventually confirmation from end customers shows promise and confidence in the tech offering. Battery performance remains important, but I have assigned it a slightly lower (still material) weight. The lower weight acknowledges some of the lab feasibility tests and early validation that have been done till now, and the experimental deployment, like in the Ducati demo, shows pending validation is important but incremental. I have also assigned a reasonably high weight to time to market, because valuations are definitely affected by a longer time to market and commercialization uncertainties. Financial runway is weighed slightly less because it impacts risk of dilution and execution delays, but is not directly a determinant of commercialization success. The final factors in my model - commercial traction, partnerships, and execution track record - build credibility and are assigned relatively minor weights.

The framework outlines what I have noted in the Progress Section already, but provides a quantitative lens to view the indicative magnitude of progress. My framework suggests QS is still far from commercialization, but has decisively moved ahead from an early feasibility stage too. The weighted average score has improved from ~78% risks open to ~56% risks open at this stage - using my conservative estimates. A meaningful portion of core risks has been reduced - especially around manufacturing direction, OEM engagement, ecosystem support, and financial survival.

I have kept some factors still similarly positioned (conservative). Time to market is one factor where, although I feel a late decade commercialization expectation is better understood now, removing uncertainties compared to where we were in 2023. However, markets may have assumed earlier commercialization in 2023, and hence my minimal change in progress numbers allows for the benefit of the doubt that should drag valuations. It is also true that high volume manufacturing proof is still pending and contracts are yet to be signed - something investors should now focus on, with several risks materially reduced.

The bridge to ~67% market cap expansion and ~22% share price increase while the risks have reduced from ~78% to ~56% is still subjective. I feel for the magnitude of progress made versus where the share is trading at now, favorably tilts entries toward opportunistic buys, especially after corrections from the 2025 tops. QS is no longer just an option in my view, which it was in 2023. Now it has a tangible blueprint and remaining risks that can be tracked far easier. That should count for more than a mere double-digit growth in share prices/valuations.

What to Watch

The remaining risks to the thesis have been highlighted throughout the thesis, but I collate them here again for discussion. While QS has demonstrated pilot level capabilities and is close to a factory stage, yield rates and cost metrics are still unknown at high volume throughput. The time to contract wins and qualification cycle uncertainties are also open. Meaningful revenues are several years out. Any delay in B-sample or C-sample validation, or factory readiness, would push revenue further out and pressure valuation, as well as raise financial or dilution risks. Detailed performance data is pending. Economics compared to lithium-ion alternatives are also a big deal - but I ignore that in my framework as of today because that is a topic that becomes more relevant closer to commercialization.

These remaining risks (a reduced list from 2023) are what will be watched in the upcoming quarters, including Q4 2025. Any manufacturing data, like throughput or yield indications, will be important to track. Production readiness on the Eagle line would materially strengthen the thesis. If Q4 misses these operational details, it could still be overlooked, but a lack of such details over the next 3-4 quarters should raise concerns.

Updates on B1 sample performance, even subjective, would be welcome. Partnerships with automotive companies, OEMs, and manufacturers should remain positive throughout this commercial readiness phase. It is also important to meet the capex guidance for the full year to be between $30-40m. And EBITDA losses are to remain between $245m-$260m. I will also await any guidance issues for the next financial year.

Overall, I don't expect Q4 to be a make or break quarter. Fresh Buys today will have to watch for progress indicators over the next 3-4 quarters and be ready to remain patient through volatility. Markets in the near term may remain cautious about narrative-based pre-revenue stocks, so investors should look at a window of at least a year from now and be comfortable riding deep losses through this period. My analysis focused on the progress made over a little over 2 years deliberately, because that kind of time lapse is long enough to cut out interim noise. Otherwise, QS has seen triple digit returns and 40-50% corrections quite a few times in the interim. The end-to-end ~22% uptick in share prices lives through this volatility and ends up looking more aligned to the magnitude of progress made.

data.title: Greystone Capital Q4 2025 Portfolio Analysis
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Greystone Capital Q4 2025 Letter To Clients.

Looking back, there were three primary contributors to underperformance during 2025, driven mostly by portfolio-specific outcomes rather than broad market conditions. First, there were instances of poor stock selection where I was wrong about business trajectory, earnings power or management quality (Sylogist (SYZ.TO), Franklin Covey (FC)). Second, several holdings that performed strongly in 2024 (Limbach (LMB), Natural Resource Partners, Medical Facilities Corp., Innovative Foods Holdings (IVFH)) experienced valuation compression or flat performance despite strong operational progress. Third, we have largely avoided AI themed investments, which proved to be a meaningful headwind versus the indices. Negative contributors were offset by strong performance from APi Group, KITS Eyecare, Bel Fuse (BELFA) and Leon’s Furniture (LNF.TO).

In hindsight, I could have prevented some performance detraction by reducing our exposure to certain investments that had appreciated strongly going into and during the year, but in more than a decade of managing capital, my willingness to stand by our investments so long as the fundamental story hasn’t changed, has indeed been the right approach. That’s because this strategy has minimized our investment mistakes and has allowed for the efficient compounding of your capital.

Importantly, underperformance at the portfolio level did not equate to poor business performance. Several holdings delivered record operating results while their share prices failed to reflect that progress. Natural Resource Partners finished the year roughly flat despite a very strong year of free cash flow generation and debt reduction, bringing us one step closer to increased distributions. Medical Facilities declined modestly despite substantial share repurchases, asset monetization, and continued cash generation, and Limbach finished the year down significantly despite record results and continued execution in its higher-quality ODR segment.

As a result of both mistakes and opportunity cost, throughout the year, we exited the positions where our original thesis no longer held and reallocated capital toward businesses with higher quality, more visible earnings power, and more attractive forward return profiles. While this creates the appearance of selling after periods of weak performance, these decisions were driven by relative risk-reward and long-term expected returns, not short-term price action. The cumulative effect of these decisions is a portfolio built around fewer variables and clearer drivers of long-term value.

To that end, attractive returns from here do not require heroic assumptions or perfect execution, only that the world continues to function as it already does. While none of our businesses are flawless, each is deeply embedded in critical systems, supported by recurring demand, aligned incentives, and a demonstrated ability to grow per-share value over time. Our approach is to allow these businesses to compound through short-term noise and periodic volatility, with the underlying bet being a straightforward one; global coal usage will remain durable over the next 3–5 years, access to cost-effective eyecare will continue to expand, commercial buildings will require ongoing fire and life-safety maintenance, and hospitals will continue performing surgical procedures.

Although our concentrated portfolio will inevitably experience bouts of volatility, I feel very good about the odds of delivering a satisfactory long-term outcome from today’s starting point. My family and I remain large investors in Greystone, firmly aligning our interests.

Portfolio Commentary

At year end, our top five holdings, representing nearly 75% of our capital, consist of Natural Resource Partners, KITS Eyecare, our undisclosed position, APi Group and Medical Facilities Corp.

At the risk of sounding like a broken record, the current opportunity set remains unusually attractive. In addition to the undisclosed position discussed below, we initiated a new core holding that I believe can deliver compelling long-term returns during the next few years, which I discuss in the Appendix at the end of this letter.

We also established two smaller positions in very different businesses, each characterized by durable competitive advantages, strong free-cash-flow generation and valuations that remain reasonable relative to their long-term prospects. I look forward to disclosing more over time.

Top Five Positions

Undisclosed Position

Apologies for the opaqueness here, but we are still actively buying this stock, and I have not disclosed anything publicly yet. In addition, Greystone Capital is currently raising capital for a Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV) to invest in this business.

This market-leading, asset-light service business benefits from meaningful geographic and regulatory barriers that support stable, recession-resistant cash flows and allow reinvestment at mid-teens to low-twenties returns. Despite predictable free cash flow, limited competition, and a balance sheet that enables both aggressive share repurchases and disciplined reinvestment, the company continues to trade as a lower-quality, cyclical service provider. I believe this mispricing reflects an outdated view of the business rather than its current reality. Even assuming no multiple expansion, ongoing free cash flow generation, reinvestment, and capital returns support attractive forward returns from today’s valuation.

What makes this opportunity especially attractive is the combination of underappreciated compounding characteristics and a management team that understands capital allocation at a very high level. The purpose of the SPV is to build a concentrated position in this business at a meaningful discount to intrinsic value. Please feel free to reach out for more information.

Natural Resource Partners (NRP)

Natural Resource Partners, our coal royalty business, delivered another strong year in 2025, continuing its evolution into a debt-free, capital-return-focused royalty and infrastructure business. Due to the nature of the royalty business model that avoids ongoing capital expenditures and high operating costs, cash flows remained robust across the mineral rights segment, in what has been a tough environment for both met and thermal coal prices. During 2025, global coal consumption of 8.8 billion tons once again reached record highs, and given the severe supply/demand imbalance globally, I’d imagine pricing conditions begin to change favorably as we approach the end of the decade.

In my view, the most underappreciated attribute of NRP’s business is lack of price risk being taken here. The royalty business model provides exceptional durability, having been stress-tested through various pricing environments and macro environments. Most importantly, the balance sheet continues to strengthen, and leverage metrics now sit comfortably within long-term targets, giving management flexibility to increase shareholder distributions within a few quarters. With a 20-year track record of positive free cash flow and plenty of reserves in the ground, I like our odds to collect distributions from NRP well into the foreseeable future.

As a reminder, we have no way of forecasting the price of coal, and we are not underwriting heroic growth assumptions. NRP is positioned to generate significant free cash flow during the next several years, much of which should be returned to us. We also get to capture the upside of any exuberance in coal prices along with the eventual recovery of soda ash. Shares trade at a substantial discount to my intrinsic value estimate of $200-250/share, making NRP one of the most compelling asymmetries in the portfolio.

KITS Eyecare (KITS.TO)

KITS Eyecare, our e-commerce eyecare business, continued to execute exceptionally well through 2025, further validating the strength of its vertically integrated, direct-to-consumer eyecare model. KITS is exactly the type of business we want to own through volatility: founder-led, operationally disciplined and focused on long-term value creation rather than near-term optics. While valuation will occasionally invite debate, our focus remains on the durability of the cash flows moving forward. KITS is an innovative, customer-focused business that has built a better, and more cost-effective model for purchasing glasses and contacts. Despite low brand awareness, especially in the US, KITS has found product/market/fit, and 2026 is on pace to be another record year of revenue and EBITDA.

Despite being just eight years old as a business, KITS is one of the fastest organically growing businesses in Canada, and has achieved the holy grail of e-commerce, selling into a huge TAM combined with strong unit economics in the form of an incredibly favorable CAC/LTV ratio. KITS refusal to participate in expensive, highly competitive marketing channels has allowed them to spread the word organically and in select markets across Canada and the US, clearing the way for profitable growth even as revenue accelerates 25-30% per year. Customer focus and cost discipline are woven into the culture of the business, enabling KITS to stand out from both service and price perspectives. High quality prescription glasses with lens upgrades retail on KITS.com for less than $60. Similar products at competing brands would cost between $150 – $300.

The structural advantages of KITS business model became increasingly evident in 2025. In-house manufacturing, lens edging, and fulfillment enable tighter quality control and faster inventory turns, while proprietary customer data improves marketing efficiency and lifetime value. The result is a model with high incremental margins, low working capital intensity and strong cash generation. KITS remains largely insulated from technological disruption and macro volatility, as vision correction is non-discretionary and repeat-driven. Management continues to reinvest prudently in automation, logistics, and new styles, extending the company’s competitive moat while preserving attractive unit economics. As a result, KITS continues to take share from both traditional optometry and less integrated online competitors, which I expect to continue well into the future.

At current prices, KITS trades at a premium to traditional retailers, but I believe this understates the durability and long-term potential of the model. The company is still early in its margin and cash flow inflection, and I expect earnings growth to materially outpace revenue growth over the next several years as fixed costs are leveraged, and as high margin glasses revenue becomes a larger part of total revenue. If management continues executing as it has, KITS has the potential to compound free cash flow at very high rates over a multi-year horizon. Based on my intrinsic value estimate of approximately CAD $35-$45/share, I still see a wide disconnect between price and value.

APi Group (APG)

APi Group continues to execute as a steady, high-quality business operating in mission-critical fire and life-safety services. 2025 was another strong year, characterized by mid-single-digit organic growth, disciplined pricing, expanding margins, and robust cash generation. End-market demand remains healthy, and APG continues to shift the revenue mix toward recurring inspection, monitoring, and service work, which carries higher margins, lower working capital intensity, and strong customer stickiness. APG remains one of the most economically resilient models we own with recurring revenue, critical infrastructure exposure, and zero technological displacement risk.

The balance sheet has returned to a position of strength following the Chubb (CB) acquisition and subsequent deleveraging, once again enabling the company to pursue disciplined, bolt-on M&A while continuing to grow through organic initiatives. This, along with cash generation, a long runway to reinvest, and favorable working capital dynamics makes APG recession resistant and worthy of a high multiple.

As a result, the market has finally given APG some credit for business strength and quality. Today, APG trades at 21x free cash flow and 18.5x EBITDA. While this doesn’t appear headline cheap for a single digit organic grower, I view forward returns as primarily earnings-driven rather than multiple-driven, which is exactly the type of profile I prefer. If APG achieves their three-year targets of $10B in revenues, $1.6 billion in EBITDA and 80% FCF conversion by 2028, shares should be worth $65 at current multiples for roughly 60% upside. That equates to an 18% annual return profile with limited risk, an underappreciated setup in today’s market.

Medical Facilities Corp. (DR.TO)

We’ve owned Medical Facilities Corporation for a few years and have done very well on the back of a cash generative portfolio of hospitals and a management team that is manically focused on repurchasing shares. It’s very rare to see a management team both recognize the best use of excess capital and then stick to a plan to either reinvest it or return it to shareholders. This is one such group, and as a result I believe there remains further upside.

Medical Facilities’ business model remains fundamentally attractive, consisting of ownership interests in specialized surgical hospitals with strong local market positions, high physician alignment, and favorable long-term demographic tailwinds. These assets generate durable cash flows with limited technological displacement risk and modest ongoing capital requirements. Management has remained focused on optimizing existing assets rather than pursuing dilutive growth, and then harvesting cash flow for capital returns, a strategy that maximizes per-share value over time.

During 2025, improving surgical volumes, stable physician engagement, and disciplined cost management, combined with normalization of elective procedure demand and improved labor dynamics supported margin stabilization and strong cash flow generation. As mentioned, MFC continues to allocate capital with an owner’s mindset, buying back over 20% of shares outstanding through a tender offer and open market purchases. During the past four years, MFC has reduced shares outstanding by nearly 40%.

Despite these positives, at $11.50/share, MFC trades well below my estimate of intrinsic value between $18-20/share. As cash flow generation continues and more shares are repurchased, I believe shares will continue to climb higher, with optionality for further asset sales within the portfolio. As a reminder, MFC sold one of their hospitals, Black Hills Surgical, in November of 2024, using the proceeds to repurchase $65M in stock through a tender offer. Another deal of that nature would benefit us tremendously, but even without one, we will continue to own a cheap, cash-generative asset with a management team repurchasing plenty of stock.

Disclaimer: Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Investing involves risks which clients should be prepared to bear, including but not limited to partial or complete loss of principal originally invested.Investing in small and microcap companies can result in additional volatility and higher risk due to comparatively low market capitalization, more sensitivity to economic and market conditions, and more limited managerial and financial resources. In addition, small companies typically trade in lower volume, making them more difficult to purchase or sell at the desired time and price or in the desired amount. Please refer to Form ADV Part 2 brochure for more information about Greystone Capital Management and its personnel.
